source.title: Juvenile social play and the development of socio-cognitive skills in male rats: Does everyone benefit?

confidence_rationale: Directly evidences the failure-mode half of the candidate cluster: even with abundant play input, a stable minority (~20%) fail to acquire the socio-cognitive benefit and show the play-deprived neural signature (mPFC under-pruning). This is the 'lock onto a maladaptive calibration despite adequate environmental input' prediction. Confidence moderate: single primary study, male-only, mechanism of the individual difference unidentified.
failure_signatures: ~20.4% of richly-played rats escalate play into aggression and show reduced mPFC pruning despite equivalent play frequency and tactics -- a model in which adequate environmental input guarantees adaptive calibration is falsified.

The incompetent subgroup did not differ in juvenile play frequency or defensive tactics, so the divergence is not explained by input quantity -- a purely input-driven (no endogenous-set-point) calibration model is unsupported.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Reared with abundant rough-and-tumble play, 20.4% of male rats nonetheless escalated play into aggression in adulthood and showed larger mPFC layer-III dendritic arbors and greater distal spine density (reduced pruning) -- neural/behavioural traits paralleling play-deprived animals -- despite equivalent juvenile play exposure.
mapping.ree_translation: Adequate environmental input does not guarantee adaptive calibration of the commitment system; a stable subset locks a maladaptive configuration. This is the INV-075 lock-arm failure mode (lock onto a bad calibration) and the substrate of environmentally-determined-yet-individually-variable personality: the window and the lock exist, but the value the lock fixes is not a deterministic function of input quantity.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Rat play-fighting and mPFC pruning, not human personality or BG loop gains; the 'maladaptive lock' reading is REE's interpretation of an under-pruning phenotype, and the paper does not identify whether the divergence is endogenous predisposition, micro-experiential, or stochastic.
mapping.source_context: 54 male Long-Evans rats reared in large social groups (rich play) weaning-to-adulthood; play scoring, adult social-competence test, Golgi mPFC dendritic morphology.

confidence_components.notes: Single recent primary study, male-only, modest n; the individual-difference mechanism is unresolved (the authors call for follow-up). Mapping to 'maladaptive lock / environmentally-determined personality variance' is apt but interpretive.
